1.synthesis:
The construction is complete; now the Lord speaks again to Moses, giving the precise instructions for erecting the tabernacle. The timing is specified: the first day of the first month of the second year (Exod 40:2) — exactly nine months after the Sinai covenant had been given (third month, first year, Exod 19:1), and one year after the Exodus. The new year was to begin with the erection of God's dwelling-place: the first act of Israel's second year was to install the sanctuary of the Lord.

The sequence of installation is specified in exact order: the ark with the veil-screen (v 3), the table and its utensils (v 4), the lampstand (v 4), the incense altar before the ark (v 5), the entrance screen (v 5), the burnt-offering altar at the entrance (v 6), the laver between tent and altar (v 7), the courtyard with its entrance screen (v 8). Then Moses was to anoint everything with the holy anointing oil, consecrating it as most holy (vv 9-11), and wash Aaron and his sons, clothe them, and anoint them to consecrate them as priests (vv 12-15).

Gill observes that the erection of the tabernacle waited for express divine authorization: "though the work of the tabernacle was finished, and every thing ready for rearing, and the people, no doubt, were very desirous to see it up, yet Moses will not erect it till he has express orders for doing so." Matthew Henry adds the general principle: "It is good to see God going before us in every step." The tabernacle was not assembled by human initiative — the divine order specified when and how, and Moses waited for it.

The anointing of everything before use — altar, laver, tent, furnishings — extended the consecration beyond the mere physical construction to the spiritual claiming of each object for sacred use. Nothing was used for worship before it had been set apart by the anointing oil. The principle is explicit in verse 13: "You shall anoint him, and he shall be holy." The anointing created the holiness that enabled the use, not the use that generated the holiness.
1.historical_context: The date specification — first of Nisan, the first month of the second year — made the tabernacle's erection coincide with the anniversary of the Exodus (which began on the fourteenth of Nisan, one year earlier). The tabernacle erected on the new year's first day was the covenant people's annual declaration that their life was organized around God's dwelling among them. The ordering of furniture installation (ark first, then table and lampstand, then altars and laver and court) reversed the physical construction sequence but followed the theological sequence: most holy first, least holy last — the inside-out holiness of the covenant moving from center to circumference.
1.literary_structure: The instruction unit (vv 1-15) mirrors in miniature the full structure of chs 25-31: furnishings (vv 3-5), altars (vv 6-7), court (v 8), and priestly consecration (vv 9-15). The sequence covers the full gradient from ark to courtyard to priests, providing the installation blueprint that ch 40:16-33 then executes. Each instruction is followed immediately in the execution account, creating a tight command-obedience structure at this final stage.
1.theological_application: Moses's patient waiting for divine authorization before erecting the tabernacle — even though everything was ready — models the principle that readiness does not equal authorization. The church may have all the gifts, materials, and human resources for a given work but should still wait for divine direction before proceeding. Matthew Henry's comment — "It is good to see God going before us in every step" — speaks against the presumption that identifies human capability with divine call. The anointing that consecrated each object before use speaks to the NT principle of Spirit-filling before service: "Do not leave Jerusalem, but wait for the gift my Father promised" (Acts 1:4). Even Jesus waited for the Spirit's anointing before beginning his public ministry.
1.christology: The specification of the first day of the first month for the tabernacle's erection — new year's day — placed God's dwelling at the beginning of Israel's year, the first thing in the calendar of the new year. This is the pattern of Christ's lordship: "He is before all things, and in him all things hold together" (Col 1:17). The anointing of the tabernacle and all its furnishings with the holy oil points to the Spirit's consecration of Christ — "God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power" (Acts 10:38) — before his public ministry began. The anointing of Aaron and his sons immediately after the tabernacle's consecration foreshadows Christ's consecration of his disciples for priestly ministry through the Spirit: "As the Father has sent me, even so I am sending you... Receive the Holy Spirit" (John 20:21-22).

16.synthesis:
"Thus Moses did; according to all that the Lord commanded him, so he did" (Exod 40:16). With this comprehensive obedience-declaration, the execution of the divine installation begins. The tabernacle was erected on the first day of the first month of the second year. Moses placed the testimony in the ark (v 20), set the mercy seat on top (v 20), brought the ark into the tabernacle and hung the screening veil (v 21), placed the table on the north side and set the bread in order (v 22-23), set the lampstand on the south side and lit the lamps (vv 24-25), placed the gold altar before the veil and burned fragrant incense on it (vv 26-27), hung the entrance screen (v 28), set the burnt-offering altar at the entrance and offered the burnt offering and grain offering (v 29), placed the laver between tent and altar and filled it with water for washing (vv 30-32), and set up the courtyard with its screen (v 33).

Matthew Henry observes the precision of Moses's obedience: "When the tabernacle and the furniture of it were prepared, they did not put off the rearing of it till they came to Canaan, though they now hoped to be there very shortly." The temptation to defer sacred installation until a more permanent, more convenient, more settled location might have made pastoral sense — but Moses did not defer. The wilderness was where God had commanded it, and so the wilderness was where it went up.

The installation of the ark with the testimony (v 20) is the first substantive act. Everything proceeds from there: the mercy seat covers the testimony, the veil screens the ark, the furniture fills the holy place around it, the altars serve the courtyard. The sequence moves outward from the most holy center — the ark and testimony — to the outermost boundary of the court. The holy of holies determined the order of everything else.

Every object was placed exactly as commanded: the table on the north (v 22), the lampstand on the south (v 24), the incense altar before the veil (v 26), the burnt-offering altar at the entrance (v 29), the laver between tent and altar (v 30). The positions were not incidental but theological — a spatial argument about holiness, approach, and worship arranged in stone and linen and wood.
16.historical_context: The installation took place on a single day — the first of Nisan — which was significant as the beginning of Israel's sacred calendar. The acts of installation (erecting frames, hanging curtains, placing furniture, anointing, lighting, burning incense, offering sacrifice, filling the laver) would have required the entire day, beginning at dawn and ending at sunset, with all the assembly of Israel watching from their camp positions around the newly erected sanctuary. The daily rhythm immediately began: the lampstand lit, the incense offered, the continual burnt offering initiated. From day one the sanctuary was not merely erected but operational.
16.literary_structure: The installation narrative (vv 16-33) is the most precise execution-account in the book: each object is named, its place specified, and the action performed catalogued. The repeated "as the Lord had commanded Moses" (vv 19, 21, 23, 25, 27, 29, 32) — a seven-fold echo of ch 39's same refrain — declares total obedience at every step. The climax is verse 33: "So Moses finished the work." The parallel with Genesis 2:1-2 ("Thus the heavens and the earth were finished... and on the seventh day God finished his work") is unmistakable. Moses finishing the tabernacle's work is a new-creation moment.
16.theological_application: Matthew Henry's point about not deferring sacred installation until more convenient times speaks to the temptation to condition obedience on circumstances: "I'll worship more faithfully when I have more time, more space, more stability." Moses erected the tabernacle in the wilderness — the least stable, least comfortable possible location — because God commanded it there. The principle for the church is the same: the call to worship is not conditional on settled circumstances but is the very thing that stabilizes the community in the midst of wilderness. The sanctuary created the center around which Israel ordered its wilderness camp; the church's gathered worship creates the center around which believers orient their scattered lives.
16.christology: The seven-fold "as the Lord had commanded Moses" in this passage echoes the seven-fold obedience refrain of ch 39's garment-making — and both point toward the one who said, "I have come to do your will, O God" (Heb 10:7). Moses finishing the work of the tabernacle is the OT counterpart to Jesus's "It is finished" — the completion of the Father-appointed work that prepares the dwelling of divine glory. The lampstand lit on the first day of the tabernacle's existence corresponds to the Spirit poured out on the church's first day at Pentecost — the permanent illumination of the new dwelling of God. The sacrifices offered immediately (v 29) correspond to the ongoing worship of the church before the heavenly altar: "Through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God" (Heb 13:15).

34.synthesis:
"Then the cloud covered the tent of meeting, and the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle. And Moses was not able to enter the tent of meeting because the cloud settled on it, and the glory of the Lord filled the tabernacle" (Exod 40:34-35). The book of Exodus ends here — not with legislation, not with narrative, not with human speech, but with the overwhelming manifestation of divine glory in the structure the people have built at God's command. All the instructions, all the offerings, all the craftsmanship, all the priestly garments, all the sacrifices and anointings were for this: that God would fill his house with his presence.

Gill explains the cloud's form: "not erect as a pillar, but more expanded, so as to cover the tabernacle without." The same cloud that had guided Israel since Egypt — the pillar of cloud by day, pillar of fire by night (Exod 13:21) — now descended in a new configuration, enveloping the entire structure. And inside, simultaneously, the glory of the Lord — the kābôd YHWH, the Shekinah — filled every chamber. Even Moses, who had gone in before the Lord throughout the forty days on Sinai and had spoken with God "face to face," could not enter. The glory was not reserved for him alone; it filled the whole house.

Matthew Henry's comment is one of the most celebrated in all his commentary: "The Shechinah, the divine eternal Word, though not yet made flesh, yet, as a prelude to that event, came and dwelt among them, John i. 14." The cloud filling the tabernacle was the prelude to the incarnation: God practicing, as it were, the mode of dwelling with his people that would reach its completion when "the Word became flesh and dwelt (eskēnōsen, 'tabernacled') among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father" (John 1:14).

The concluding verses explain the tabernacle's ongoing function in the wilderness: when the cloud lifted from the tabernacle, Israel set out; when it did not lift, they did not set out (Exod 40:36-37). The cloud of the Lord was on the tabernacle by day, fire by night, in the sight of all the house of Israel throughout all their journeys (Exod 40:38). The dwelling of God guided the movement of the people. The presence of God was not merely a spiritual comfort but the practical director of all Israel's travel. Where the cloud led, Israel followed; where it rested, Israel rested.
34.historical_context: The divine glory filling Solomon's temple at its dedication (1 Kgs 8:10-11) repeats the Exodus 40 scene almost exactly: "the cloud filled the house of the Lord, so that the priests could not stand to minister because of the cloud, for the glory of the Lord filled the house of the Lord." The pattern established at the tabernacle's inauguration became the template for every subsequent divine dwelling. The glory's departure from the temple is recorded in Ezekiel 10-11 as the supreme sign of covenant judgment; its return in Ezekiel 43 is the supreme sign of covenant restoration. The beginning and end of the tabernacle narrative are thus the beginning and end of the entire OT theology of God's presence among his people.
34.literary_structure: The final five verses of Exodus form the book's theological climax and structural resolution. Everything from chapter 1's suffering in Egypt to chapter 40's glory-filling has moved toward this moment: God dwelling in the midst of his redeemed people. The cloud-and-fire guidance (vv 36-38) provides the practical conclusion, explaining how the tabernacle would function in the ongoing wilderness journey. The book ends not with arrival but with readiness: the dwelling built, the glory present, the people poised to follow wherever the cloud leads.
34.theological_application: That Moses himself could not enter the tabernacle when the glory filled it is a declaration about the distance between even the greatest human mediator and the fullness of divine presence. The glory of God is not an achievement of human devotion or priestly skill; it is a divine gift that fills what has been faithfully prepared but cannot be commanded or contained by the human builders. The church that has faithfully built according to the divine pattern — Word-centered, prayer-filled, sacrament-celebrating, love-practicing — creates the conditions in which the Spirit may fill; but it cannot manufacture the filling. "Not by might, nor by power, but by my Spirit, says the Lord of hosts" (Zech 4:6). The tabernacle's filling was followed by decades of wilderness wandering — glory does not guarantee ease, and cloud-guidance does not mean settled comfort. But it does mean that the people were never alone in the wilderness.
34.christology: Matthew Henry's identification of the tabernacle-filling as "a prelude to that event" — the incarnation — is the Christological key to the entire book of Exodus. The cloud and glory at the tabernacle's inauguration is the first-fruits of what God intended from before creation: to dwell among his people. John 1:14's eskēnōsen ("tabernacled") is a direct invocation of this passage: the glory that filled the wilderness tent filled the flesh of the incarnate Word permanently, fully, and in person. Where Moses could not enter because the glory was too great, Christ is himself the glory: "the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature" (Heb 1:3). The Shekinah that hovered above the mercy seat between the cherubim now speaks from within a human body in a Galilean village — and ultimately from a cross outside Jerusalem, and from the throne of heaven where he makes intercession forever. The book of Exodus ends with the cloud resting on the tabernacle and fire illuminating the camp through all their journeys. The book of Revelation ends with the same vision completed: "Behold, the dwelling place of God is with man. He will dwell with them, and they will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God" (Rev 21:3). The tabernacle at Sinai was the promise; the new Jerusalem is the fulfillment.
